target.name: Hermeus
target.founder: AJ Piplica, Skyler Shuford, Glenn Case, Mike Smayda

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Supersonic & Hypersonic (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 1 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Supersonic & Hypersonic, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Hermeus, a Series C stage company developing supersonic and hypersonic flight technology, is valued at $1.0B post-money on $500M total capital raised. The supersonic and hypersonic sector comprises a sparse comp universe of 3 total peers—2 public (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 1 private (Boom Supersonic)—limiting the applicability of traditional peer-median approaches.

Given the narrow and heterogeneous peer set, stage-progression analysis provides the primary valuation signal. Applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C to next-stage progression yields $1.7B. This method is the only applicable triangulation approach, as peer-median analysis cannot be reliably anchored to fewer than three same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables. The isolation of the Supersonic & Hypersonic sector makes cross-sector median inference misleading.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $1.4B to $2.0B, with a median of $1.7B. This range reflects the stage-progression methodology applied to the current $1.0B post-money valuation. Key uncertainties include the absence of sufficient same-stage peers to validate relative positioning, the heterogeneity of the three available comparables, and the nascent regulatory and commercial status of the underlying hypersonic and supersonic markets. Hermeus's valuation depends materially on execution risk and market adoption—factors not fully captured by stage-based mechanics alone.
